carry_forwards: handlers.py is ~290 lines (one handler per verb); if it grows, split by verb group (e.g. lifecycle vs discovery vs plan) - the registry makes that a mechanical move.
A future new verb should ship with its handler + a test; the catalog coverage test already pins that every verb is dispatchable.
The Context object is currently a plain dataclass; if handlers start needing more shared helpers (e.g. lazy ws caching), grow Context rather than passing extra args.
No characterization snapshot test was added (the existing suite sufficed); revisit only if a future output-shape change needs a tighter guard.

done_when.clause: cli.py's responsibilities are separated into coherent, navigable units
done_when.evidence: lume/cli/ package: catalog.py (verbs+USAGE, 70), flags.py (argv parsing, 35), io.py (emitters + human/JSON renderers, 140), context.py (Context, 39), handlers.py (per-verb handlers + registry, ~290), app.py (thin main, ~98). Each file serves one concern.

done_when.clause: Adding or changing a verb is local rather than threading a long function
done_when.evidence: A verb = one catalog entry (catalog.py) + one handle_<verb>(ctx) co-locating its validation/action/output (handlers.py) + one HANDLERS row. No edits to a 320-line dispatch function; the old per-command pre-validation pass is gone.

app.main() is a ~50-line body: parse flags -> membership check -> backing selection -> build Context -> HANDLERS.get(cmd, handle_transition) -> dispatch inside one try/except LumeError. The 16-block if-chain is gone.

done_when.clause: External CLI contract + all outputs byte-for-byte unchanged; suite passes with only mechanical edits
done_when.evidence: 291 tests pass with NO edits to any existing assertion (only code moved). Live spot-checks match prior output: human queue/detail, verbs --json (20), structured errors, exit codes 0/1/2 (usage->2, gate->1, unknown->2). Public surface (lume.cli.main/_CATALOG/_VERB_NAMES) and bin/lume unchanged.

overall_verdict: Net positive. cli.py (591 lines, one growing if-chain) is now a focused lume/cli/ package with a per-verb handler registry, and main() is thin routing. The maintainability goal is met on the human axis the operator raised: a reader navigates by concern, and adding/changing a verb is a local edit (catalog entry + handler) rather than threading a long function. The whole refactor was behaviour-preserving with zero test-assertion changes - the existing suite (which drives main() and pins stdout/stderr/exit codes) made that guarantee cheap and verifiable. Splitting relocation (E1) from restructuring (E2) kept every diff reviewable. Total line count rose slightly (helpers + handler boilerplate), but per-file size and cohesion - the thing that was actually hard for a human - improved sharply.

stage_verdicts.cost: One analysis iteration, no engine code.
stage_verdicts.saved: Pinned the pain precisely: the helpers were already fine; the problem was main() (320-line if-chain) and a verb's concerns scattered across catalog/validation/dispatch. That focus stopped the refactor from over-reaching into code that didn't need it.

Chose the handler registry over a cosmetic module split, and surfaced the load-bearing constraint up front: lume.cli.main/_CATALOG/_VERB_NAMES must stay importable (tests + bin), so the package __init__ re-exports them. Sized the work into two behaviour-preserving slices.

stage_verdicts.cost: Two iterations: package split (relocation), then Context + handler registry.
stage_verdicts.saved: Splitting relocation (E1) from restructuring (E2) kept each diff reviewable and the suite green between them. The existing suite - which drives main() and asserts stdout/stderr/exit codes - was a complete regression net: 291 tests passed at every step with zero assertion edits. A single try/except LumeError in main collapsed ~6 scattered error catches into one (all errors subclass LumeError).
Honest done-when check + carry-forwards; closes on the engine's own verbs.
